citation.sourceDocument: Nebraska DOR 2025 Tax Calculation Schedule + 1040N standard deduction; LB 754 cuts the 2026 top rate to 4.55% (Neb. Rev. Stat. §77-2715.03)
citation.sourceNote: Nebraska levies a graduated tax (Neb. Rev. Stat. §77-2715.03) on Nebraska taxable income, which the wage engine models as Nebraska AGI (≈ federal AGI for wage income) minus the Nebraska standard deduction. The 2023 LB 754 reform set a multi-year rate-reduction path that cuts the top bracket to 5.84% (2024), 5.20% (2025), 4.55% (2026), and 3.99% (2027); for 2026 the statute drops BOTH of the schedule's top two rates (the 2025 schedule's 5.01% and 5.20% bands) to 4.55%, merging them into a single top band, so the four-bracket 2025 schedule becomes a clean THREE-bracket 2026 schedule — 2.46% / 3.51% / 4.55%. The two lower rates (2.46%, 3.51%) and all bracket thresholds are read verbatim from the official 2025 Nebraska Tax Calculation Schedule (Form 8-460-2025): single 2.46% to $4,030, 3.51% to $24,120, 4.55% above; married filing jointly / qualifying surviving spouse 2.46% to $8,040, 3.51% to $48,250, 4.55% above; head of household 2.46% to $7,510, 3.51% to $38,590, 4.55% above. The base-tax figures reconcile to the cent (the single 4.55% band opens at $24,120 with a cumulative base of $804.30 = 2.46%·4,030 + 3.51%·20,090). The 2026 RATE (4.55%) is the statutory LB 754 value — definitively in effect, not a forecast — while the bracket thresholds and the standard deduction use the latest-published official 2025 figures, because the Nebraska DOR had not yet posted the 2026 indexed Tax Calculation Schedule at retrieval (it publishes late in the year); the thresholds index up ~2-3% annually, so using the 2025 thresholds under-indexes slightly and errs slightly HIGH — the conservative side, the California/Vermont precedent. The standard deduction is $8,600 (single / married-filing-separately), $17,200 (married jointly / surviving spouse), $12,600 (head of household), from the 2025 Form 1040N instructions. Married filing separately maps to single exactly (Nebraska's MFS schedule and $8,600 deduction match single); a qualifying surviving spouse falls back to the married-jointly schedule and $17,200 deduction. Nebraska's relief for personal exemptions is a nonrefundable PERSONAL EXEMPTION CREDIT (~$171 per filer and per dependent for 2026, Neb. Rev. Stat. §77-2716.01), not a deduction; the engine has no flat-credit mechanism, so it is omitted at launch fidelity — a flat overstatement of ~$171 per filer, the conservative direction, consistent with how Oregon's exemption credit and every other state's credits are deferred. The Nebraska child/dependent care credit, the earned-income credit, the now-fully-exempt Social Security income, the credit for the property-tax-paid (Form PTC), and Nebraska's other credits are omitted at launch fidelity; Nebraska levies no local/municipal income tax. Worked example, single $60,000 wages: Nebraska taxable = 60,000 − 8,600 = $51,400; tax = 2.46%·4,030 + 3.51%·(24,120 − 4,030) + 4.55%·(51,400 − 24,120) = 99.14 + 705.16 + 1,241.24 = $2,045.54 (before the omitted ~$171 exemption credit). Verified against the official 2025 Nebraska Tax Calculation Schedule and Form 1040N booklet and Neb. Rev. Stat. §77-2715.03, and cross-checked against the Tax Foundation 2026 state-rate table on 2026-06-08.
